content: Enviroment нғӯ JVM, Spark, Resource, Hadoop, System, ClasspathмҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ м„Өм • м •ліҙл“Өмқ„ н•ңлҲҲм—җ нҷ•мқён•  мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” Environment нғӯмқҙлӢӨ. мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ мҡҙмҳҒмқҙ кё°ліёмқ„ л„ҳм–ҙ лӢӨм–‘н•ң кІғл“Өмқ„ мӢңлҸ„н•ҳкі мһҗ н•  л•Ң м„Өм •мқ„ кұҙл“ңлҰ°лӢӨ. мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ мҳөм…ҳмқҳ м„Өм •мқҖ 1) м„Өм • нҢҢмқјмқ„ нҶөн•ҙ(SPARK_HOMEм—җ мң„м№ҳ) 2) spark-submit мӢң вҖ“confмҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ мҳөм…ҳмқ„ нҶөн•ҙ 3) Spark м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳ лӮҙмқҳ SparkSessionмқ„ нҶөн•ҙм„ң м„Өм •
title: Spark UI

content: Executors нғӯ мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬лҠ” executor-cores, executor-memoryмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ executor лӢЁмң„лЎң мһҗмӣҗмқ„ н• лӢ№н•ҳкё°м—җ н•ҙлӢ№ л¶Җл¶„кіј кҙҖл Ёлҗҳм–ҙм„ң м„Өм •мқ„ мЎ°м •н•  л•Ң мӮ¬мҡ©н•ңлӢӨ. к·ёл Үкё°м—җ м•„лһҳмқҳ л©”нҠёлҰӯм—җ мһҲм–ҙм„ңлҸ„ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬мҷҖ кҙҖл Ёлҗң к°’л“Өмқҙ мЈјлҘј мқҙлЈЁлҠ” кІғмқ„ ліј мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ нҠңлӢқ мӢң, shuffleмқҳ IOмҷҖ н•Ёк»ҳ executorмқҳ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ мЈјмҡ” мҡ”мҶҢлЎң л§Һмқҙ лӢӨлӨ„м§Җкё°м—җ, к·ёлҹ° мғҒнҷ©м—җм„ңлҸ„ мң„мҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ мҲҳм№ҳк°Җ м–ҙл–Ө л¶Җл¶„мқ„ лӮҳнғҖлӮҙ мЈјлҠ” м§Җ м•Ңкі  мһҲлҠ” кІғмқҖ л§Өмҡ° мӨ‘мҡ”н•ҳлӢӨ.
title: Spark UI

content: SQL нғӯ Spark SQLлЎң мҲҳн–үлҗҳлҠ” мһ‘м—…мқҖ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ SQL нғӯм—җ QueryлҘј мғқм„ұн•ҳкІҢ лҗңлӢӨ. RDD м—°мӮ°мқҙ м•„лӢҲлқј Spark SQLм—җ кё°л°ҳн•ң мІҳлҰ¬лқјл©ҙ, Job, Stage, Task м •ліҙлҘј нҶөн•ҙ мһ‘м—…мқҳ нқҗлҰ„мқ„ нҢҢм•…н•ҳкё°лҠ” мЎ°кёҲ м–ҙл өлӢӨ. к·ёлҹ¬н•ң л¶Җл¶„м—җм„ң SQLмқҳ мҝјлҰ¬ н”Ңлһңмқ„ к·ёлһҳн”„лЎң н‘ңнҳ„н•ҳкі , details м •ліҙлҘј нҶөн•ҙ к°Ғ лӢЁкі„мқҳ м—¬лҹ¬ мҲҳм№ҳлҘј н•ң лҲҲм—җ нҷ•мқён•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. мң„мҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ Queryмқҳ Descriptionмқҳ л§ҒнҒ¬лҘј нҶөн•ҙ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ Detail нҺҳмқҙм§Җм—җ лҸ„лӢ¬н•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. Scanм—җм„ңл¶Җн„°, Exchange, Aggregate л“ұ мҝјлҰ¬ н”Ңлһңм—җ л”°лҘё DAG к·ёлһҳн”„лҘј ліҙм—¬мӨҖлӢӨ. мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ transformationм—җм„ң narrowмҷҖ wideлҘј кө¬л¶„н•ҳлҠ” кІғкіј к°ҷмқҙ, Joinм—җ л”°лҘё Shuffleмқҳ нҳ•нғңлҠ” м„ұлҠҘм—җ нҒ° мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҒјм№ңлӢӨ. к·ёлҹ¬н•ң joinмқ„ мөңм Ғнҷ”н•ҳлҠ”лҚ° н•„мҡ”н•ң м •ліҙлҠ” м•„лһҳмқҳ SQL нғӯм—җм„ң м–»мқ„ мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark UI

content: Execution plan goal An execution plan is the set of operations executed to translate a query language statement (SQL, Spark SQL, Dataframe operations etc.) to a set of optimized logical and physical operations. Execution plan is a set of operations that will be executed from the SQL(or Spark SQL) statement to the DAG which will be send to Spark Executors. Info DAG? Directed Acyclic Graph. A DAG is an acyclic graph produced by the DAG scheduler in Spark. As a graph, it is composed of vertices and edges that will represent RDDs and operations (transformations and actions) performed on them. On Spark, the optimizer is named Catalyst and can be represented by the schema below. It will produce different types of plans: Operation names are: Analysis Logical Optimization Physical Planning Cost Model Analysis Code Generation And those operations will produce various plans: Unresolved logical plan Resolved logical plan Optimized logical plan Physical plans The goal of all these operations and plans is to produce automatically the most effective way to process your query. Generating plans In order to generate plans, you have to deal with Dataframes regardless they come from SQL or raw dataframe. And the function you will use is (in Python) explain(). For example, if you have these two dataframes: itemsSchema = ("id integer, name string, price float") ordersSchema=("id integer, itemid integer, count integer") items = spark.createDataFrame([[0, "Tomato", 2.0], \ [1, "Watermelon", 5.5], \ [2, "pineapple", 7.0]], \ schema=itemsSchema) orders = spark.createDataFrame([[100, 0, 1], \ [100, 1, 1], \ [101, 2, 3], \ [102,2,8]],\ schema=ordersSchema) You can control them like this: from pyspark.sql.functions import sum y=(items.join(orders,items.id==orders.itemid, how="inner"))\ .where(items.id==2)\ .groupBy("name","price").agg(sum("count")\ .alias("c")) or like this: items.createOrReplaceTempView("ITEMS") orders.createOrReplaceTempView("ORDERS") x=sql('''select ITEMS.name, ITEMS.price, SUM(ORDERS.count) as c from ITEMS, ORDERS where ITEMS.id=ORDERS.itemid and ITEMS.id=2 group by ITEMS.name, ITEMS.price''') In both cases, you will be able to call explain() x.explain() y.explain() By default, calling explain with no argument will produce a physical plan explanation: Getting various plans Starting from Apache Spark 3.0, you have a new parameter mode that produce expected format for the plan: explain(mode=вҖқsimpleвҖқ) which will display the physical plan explain(mode=вҖқextendedвҖқ) which will display physical and logical plans (like вҖңextendedвҖқ option) explain(mode=вҖқcodegenвҖқ) which will display the java code planned to be executed explain(mode=вҖқcostвҖқ) which will display the optimized logical plan and related statistics (if they exist) explain(mode=вҖқformattedвҖқ) which will display a splitted output composed by a nice physical plan outline, and a section with each node details Plans differences As stated in the beginning of this post, various kinds of plans are generated after many operations processed by the Catalyst Optimizer:
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: First step: Unresolved Logical plan generation This plan is generated after a first check that verifies everything is correct on the syntactic field. Next, the semantic analysis is executed and will produced a first version of a logical plan where relation name and columns are not specifically resolved. This produced this kind of result:
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: Next step: Analyzed logical plan generation When the unresolved plan has been generated, it will resolve everything that is not resolved yet by accessing an internal Spark structure mentioned as Catalog in the previous schema. In this catalog, which can be assimilated to a metastore, a semantic analysis will be produced to verify data structures, schemas, types etc. and if everything goes well, the plan is marked as вҖңAnalyzed Logical PlanвҖқ and will be formatted like this: We can see here that, just after the вҖңAggregateвҖқ line, all the previously marked вҖңunresolved aliasвҖқ are now resolved and correctly typed вҖҰ specially the sum column.
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: Next step: Optimized logical plan Once the Logical plan has been produced, it will be optimized based on various rules applied on logical operations (But you have already noticed that all these operations were logical ones: filters, aggregation etc.). These logical operations will be reordered to optimize the logical plan. When the optimization ends, it will produced this kind of output: We can see in this plan, that predicates have been pushed down on the LogicalRDD to reduce the data volume processed by the join. We can mention too that filters are pushed to both data structure (one for the вҖңitemsвҖқ dataframe, and one for the вҖңordersвҖқ dataframe).
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: Then, many physical plans are generated and finally a unique physical plan is selected From the optimized logical plan, a plan that describes how it will be physically executed on the cluster will be generated. But before selecting a physical plan, the Catalyst Optimizer will generate many physical plans based on various strategies. Each physical plan will be estimated based on execution time and resource consumption projection and only one plan will be selected to be executed. Based on our example, the selected physical plan is this one (which is the one that is printed when you use explain() with default parameters)
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: Spark execution is Lazy, Execution plan generation is not a lazy thing. Calling explain() function is an operation that will produce all the stuff presented above, from the unresolved logical plan to a selection of one physical plan to execute. After that, and only after that, the physical plan is executed through one to many stages and tasks in a laziness way. As a consequence, it wonвҖҷt be possible to generate an unresolved logical plan by typing something like the code below (which includes a schema error: ids instead of id) >>> (items.join(orders,items.id==orders.itemid, how="inner"))\ ... .where(items.ids==2)\ ... .groupBy("name","price").agg(sum("count")\ ... .alias("c")).explain(mode="extended") Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module> File "/usr/spark-3.0.0/python/pyspark/sql/dataframe.py", line 1401, in __getattr__ "'%s' object has no attribute '%s'" % (self.__class__.__name__, name)) AttributeError: 'DataFrame' object has no attribute 'ids'
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: And how AQE(Adaptive Query Execution) interact with this stuff? AQE is a new feature in Spark 3.0 which enables plan changes at runtime. It collects statistics during plan execution and if Spark detects better plan during execution, it changes them at runtime. If we put this on an update of the catalyst Optimizer schema, it will give something like that: However, any changes decided during DAG execution wonвҖҷt be displayed after calling explain() function. If you want to see these changes, you will have to explore Spark UI and tracking skew partitions splits, joins changes etc. For example, if you enable AQE (which is not enabled by default), explain call will produce a hint in the physical plan display : In the explain output, you are hinted this physical plan is not the final plan, but if you have a look at the Spark UI, you will see that the SQL query plan is the final plan and optionally if the plan has been modified :
title: Spark Execution Plan

content: Introduction SparkвҖҷs execution hierarchy, from top to bottom is Job, Stage, Task. Slots are part of executors. A task is executed in a slot. But slots are rather a tool to execute tasks, not part of the execution hierarchy itself. Executors are a component of a Spark cluter, but not of the execution hierarchy. Hardware Hierarchy
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Cluster
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Driver
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Executor Cores / Slots: Each executor can be considered as servers and they have cores. A core can be considered a slot that can be used to put workload into. Each core can take one piece of work.
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Memory Each executor also has memory. But not all of it is given for Spark. On average 90% of this memory is given for Spark. At a high level, this memory is divided into Storage and Working Memory. Storage memory is where data is cached or persisted. Working memory is where Spark does all in-memory computations
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Storage Memory to store persisted objects Default configured limit - 50% of total storage
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Working Memory will be utilized by spark workloads 50% of total storage will be used for spark workloads
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Disks Each executor also has locally attached/mounted storage. RAM/SSD/NFS Drivers Better disks would ensure fast shuffling of data These disks are extremely important because - a lot of time, Spark does Shuffle(Data compared between partitions). In the intermediate stages, when data is moved around, that data goes to Disk. Faster the disk, Faster the shuffle. Software Hierarchy
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Transformation(lazy) Narrow(all the data needed for the transformation is available to CPU at the same time) Wide(data needs to be moved around nodes, require shuffle) Transformation select() filter() groupBy() agg() orderBy() join() dropDuplicates() withColumn() limit() drop()
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Action When we call an action, we start all the transformations that spark has staged. Action launches 1 or many jobs depending on the transformations. Jobs 1 Job can have many stages Stages It is a section of work that is going to be done 1 Stage can have many tasks Jobs and Stages are part of the orchestration Tasks(interact with hardware directly) Every task inside a stage does the same thing, only on another segment of the data If a task is required to do something different, it is required to be in the inside of another stage 1 Task is done by 1 core and on 1 partition Action show() count() collect() saveAsTable() write() foreach() Shuffle Shuffle happens whenever Spark canвҖҷt perform tasks on individual partitions or it needs data from other partitions for computation. In the above picture, we have 3 names in 3 partitions and we are trying to get the number of names for each first character. Getting the first character in each name can be done independently. But the groupBy task required all AвҖҷs in a single partition and hence th Shuffle. Shuffle gathers all AвҖҷs into a single partition and then takes count of individual names for each first character. During shuffle, data from each partitions is written into disks based on the Hash keys. In this case, Stage 1 writes data to disk based on the first character. Stage 2 pulls data from disks and gets the count.
title: Spark Hierarchy

content: Introduction Transformations are operations on RDDs, Dataframes, or Dataset, that produce new RDDs, Dataframes, or Datasets. Transformations are lazy evaluated, which means they are not executed until an action is called. Spark uses transformation to build a DAG(Directed Acyclic Graph) of computation that represents the sequence of steps required to compute the final result. Transformations can be categorized as either Narrow or Wide based on whether their dependency on input data partitions.
title: Spark Dependency

content: Narrow dependency(transformation) A narrow transformation is a transformation that need to operate only on a single parition of input data to produce one partition of output data. These transformations do not require shuffling of data between partitions. They can be executed on a single partition without needing to exchange data with other partitions. Here, are some examples are narrow dependencies: Map transformation: In this transformation, each input element of the parent Dataframe is transformed to a single output element of the child Dataframe. Since each output element depends on only one input element, it is a narrow dependency. Filter transformation: In this transformation, each partition of the parent Dataframe is filtered based on a predicate function, resulting in a child Dataframe with only the elements that satisfy the predicate. Since each partition of the child Dataframe depends on only one partition of the parent Dataframe, it is a narrow dependency. Union transformation: In this transformation, two Dataframes are combined to form a single Dataframe, with each partition of the child Dataframe depending on only one partition of each parent Dataframe. Since each partition of the child Dataframe depends on only one partition of each parent Dataframe, it is a narrow dependency.
title: Spark Dependency

content: Wide dependency(transformation) Wide dependency transformations are those where each partition of the output DataFrame depends on multiple partitions of the input DataFrame. This means that the transformation requires shuffling data across the network, which can be resource-intensive. Here are some examples of wide dependency transformations: groupByKey() - This transformation groups the elements of a DataFrame by key, producing a new DataFrame with one row per distinct key. Since the output partitions depend on all the input partitions with matching keys, groupByKey() is a wide dependency transformation. reduceByKey() - This transformation groups the elements of a DataFrame by key, and then applies a reduce function to each group to produce a new DataFrame. Since the output partitions depend on all the input partitions with matching keys, reduceByKey() is a wide dependency transformation. A join can be wide or narrow depending on the paritioning of the input. If the input tables are already paritioned on the join key, then each parition in the output of the join depends only on a partition in the input data and it will be a narrow dependency transformation. But if the input tables are not paritined on the join key, then there is need to shuffle the data and it will be wide dependency transformaiton.
title: Spark Dependency

content: Partition Pruning in Spark In standard database pruning means that the optimizer will avoid reading files that cannot contain the data that you are looking for. For example, SELECT * FROM students WHERE subject = 'English'; In this simple query, we are trying to match and identify records in the Students table that belong to subject English. This translates into a simple form that is a filter on top of a scan which means whole data gets scanned first and then filtered out according to the condition. Now, most query optimizers try to push down the filter from the top of the scan down as close as possible to the data source, in order to be able to avoid scanning the full data set. In the partition pruning technique, it follows the filter push down method and the data set is partitioned. Because in that case, if your query has a filter thatвҖҷs on partition columns, you can actually be able to skip complete sets of partition files. Partition pruning in Spark is a performance optimization that limits the number of files and partitions that Spark reads when querying. After partitioning the data, queries that match certain partition filter criteria improve performance by allowing Spark to only read a subset of the directories and files. When partition filters are present, the catalyst optimizer pushes down the partition filters. The scan reads only the directories that match the partition filters, thus reducing disk I/O. However, in reality data engineers donвҖҷt just execute a single query, or single filter in their queries, and the common case is that they actually have dimensional tables, small tables that they need to join with a larger fact table. So in this case, we can no longer apply static partition pruning because the filter is on one side of the join, and the table that is more appealing and more attractive to prune is on the other side of the join. So, we have a problem now. SELECT * FROM students JOIN DailyRoutine WHERE DailylRoutine.subject = 'English'; Some may suggest that we can join the dimension tables with the fact table beforehand. In this way, we can still trigger static pruning over a single table. And then, they can execute their filters in separate queries as shown below. There are obvious downsides to this approach because first we have to execute this quite expensive join. We are duplicating the data, because we have to generate another intermediate table. This table can be quite wide because we take a bunch of smaller tables that we are joining together with a large table. And not only itвҖҷs wide but itвҖҷs actually extremely difficult to manage in the face of updating the dimensional tables. So whenever we are making a change we actually have to re trigger this whole pipeline over again. Dynamic Partition Pruning in Spark In Spark SQL, users typically submit their queries from their favorite API in their favorite programming language, so we have data frames and data sets. Spark takes this query and translates it into a digestible form, which we call the logical plan of the query. During this phase, Spark optimizes the logical plan by applying a set of transformations which are rule-based transformations such as column pruning, constant folding, filter push down. And only later on, it will get to the actual physical planning of the query. During the physical planning phase spark generates an executable plan. This plan distributes the computation across clusters of many machines. Optimization at Logical Level LetвҖҷs start with the optimization opportunities that we have at the level of logical planning. Let us consider a data set that is partitioned across multiple files. And each partition will be differ by a particular color. On the opposite side, we will have a smaller table, which is a dimension table that is not necessarily partitioned. And then we have the typical scan operators on top of these data sets. Whenever we are filtering the dimension table, consider an example in which only rows that correspond to
title: Spark DPP(Dynamic Partition Pruning)

content: Info PartitionмқҖ RDDsлӮҳ DatasetлҘј кө¬м„ұн•ҳкі  мһҲлҠ” мөңмҶҢ лӢЁмң„ к°қмІҙмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. к°Ғ PartitionмқҖ м„ңлЎң лӢӨлҘё л…ёл“ңм—җм„ң л¶„мӮ° мІҳлҰ¬лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. Sparkм—җм„ңлҠ” н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ мөңмҶҢ м—°мӮ°мқ„ Taskлқјкі  н‘ңнҳ„н•ҳлҠ”лҚ°, мқҙ н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Taskм—җм„ң н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Partitionмқҙ мІҳлҰ¬лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң, н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ TaskлҠ” н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Coreк°Җ м—°мӮ° мІҳлҰ¬н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. мҰү, 1 Core = 1 Task = 1 Partitionмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. м„Өм •лҗң Partition мҲҳм—җ л”°лқј к°Ғ Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ кІ°м •лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҰ¬кі  мқҙ Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ кІ°көӯ Core лӢ№ н•„мҡ”н•ң л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ нҒ¬кё°лҘј кІ°м •н•ҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. в—јпёҸ Partition мҲҳ вҶ’ Core мҲҳ в—јпёҸ Partition нҒ¬кё° вҶ’ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ нҒ¬кё° л”°лқјм„ң, Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°мҷҖ мҲҳк°Җ Spark м„ұлҠҘм—җ нҒ° мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҜём№ҳлҠ”лҚ°, нҶөмғҒм ҒмңјлЎңлҠ” Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒҙмҲҳлЎқ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ лҚ” н•„мҡ”н•ҳкі , Partitionмқҳ мҲҳк°Җ л§Һмқ„мҲҳлЎқ Coreк°Җ лҚ” н•„мҡ”н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. в—јпёҸ м ҒмқҖ мҲҳмқҳ Partition = нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒ° Partition в—јпёҸ л§ҺмқҖ мҲҳмқҳ Partition = нҒ¬кё°к°Җ мһ‘мқҖ Partition AQEлһҖ? AQE(Adaptive Query Execution) мөңм Ғнҷ”лҠ” мқҙлҰ„ к·ёлҢҖлЎң shuffleмқҙ лҒқлӮң лӢӨмқҢ partitionмқ„ coalesce(лі‘н•©)лҘј н•ҙмЈјлҠ” кё°лҠҘмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. л„Ҳл¬ҙ л§ҺмқҖ partitionмқҖ л§ҺмқҖ taskк°Җ н•„мҡ”н•ҳкұ°лӮҳ I/OлҘј л§Һмқҙ мң л°ңн•  мҲҳ мһҲкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ м Ғм Ҳн•ң мҲҳк°Җ н•„мҡ”н•ңлҚ°, AQE кё°лҠҘмқҙ м Ғм Ҳн•ң partitionмқҳ мҲҳлҘј м •н•ҙ мӨҚлӢҲлӢӨ. AQE кё°лҠҘмқҖ, м„Өм •м—җм„ң spark.sql.adaptive.enabledмҷҖ spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabledк°Җ true мқј л•Ң мһ‘лҸҷн•©лӢҲлӢӨ. Spark 3.2 лІ„м „л¶Җн„°лҠ” default к°’мқҙ trueмқҙлҜҖлЎң мһҗлҸҷмңјлЎң м Ғмҡ©мқҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҰ¬кі  AQE кё°лҠҘмқҖ кё°ліём ҒмңјлЎң coalesceлҘј мӢӨн–үн•ҳлҜҖлЎң м¶©л¶„нһҲ л§ҺмқҖ мҲҳмқҳ partitionмқ„ м„Өм •н•ҙм•ј н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. AQEмқҳ Partition мҲҳлҠ” spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.initialPartitionNumмңјлЎң м„Өм •н•  мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ. мқҙ к°’мқҙ м„Өм •лҗҳм–ҙ мһҲм§Җ м•Ҡмңјл©ҙ spark.sql.shuffle.partitions к°’мқ„ л”°лқјк°ҖкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark AQE(Adaptive Query Execution)

content: AQEмҷҖ Query Stages AQEмқҳ кө¬нҳ„м—җ мһҲм–ҙ мӨ‘мҡ”н•ң кІ°м • мҡ”мҶҢлҠ” м–ём ң мһ¬-мөңм Ғнҷ”лҘј мҲҳн–үн•  кІғмқёк°Җм—җ лҢҖн•ң кІғ мһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ мҳӨнҚјл Ҳмқҙн„°лҠ” мЈјлЎң нҢҢмқҙн”„лқјмқё нҳ•нғңлЎң лі‘л ¬ н”„лЎңм„ёмҠӨлЎң мӢӨн–үлҗҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҹ¬лӮҳ м…”н”Ң лҳҗлҠ” лёҢлЎңл“ңмәҗмҠӨнҠё exchangeлҠ” мқҙлҹ¬н•ң нҢҢмқҙн”„лқјмқёмқ„ лҒҠмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ. мқҙ л¶Җл¶„мқҖ materialization pointsлқјкі  л¶ҲлҰ¬л©° Query statesлқјлҠ” мҡ©м–ҙлЎң л¶ҲлҰҪлӢҲлӢӨ. SELECT x, AVG(y) FROM t GROUP BY x ORDER BY avg(Y) мҳҲлҘј л“Өм–ҙ, мң„мҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ мҝјлҰ¬лҠ” м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ мҝјлҰ¬ кі„нҡҚкіј materialization point(Pipeline Breadk Point) к·ёлҰ¬кі  Query stateлҘј к°Җм§‘лӢҲлӢӨ: к°Ғ Query stateлҠ” мӨ‘к°„ кІ°кіјл¬јмқ„ materializeн•ҳкі  мқҙнӣ„мқҳ stageлҠ” л°ҳл“ңмӢң н•ҙлӢ№ query stateмқҳ лӘЁл“  лі‘л ¬ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ materialize лҗң мқҙнӣ„м—җл§Ң м§„н–үмқҙ к°ҖлҠҘн•©лӢҲлӢӨ. лӘЁл“  нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳм—җ лҢҖн•ң нҶөкі„м№ҳк°Җ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳкі  мқҙнӣ„мқҳ м—°мӮ°мқҖ м•„м§Ғ мӢңмһ‘лҗҳм§Җ м•ҠмқҖ мӢңм җмқҙкё°м—җ мқҙ materialization pointлҠ” мһҗм—°м ҒмңјлЎң л§Өмҡ° мўӢмқҖ мһ¬-мөңм Ғнҷ” м§Җм җмқҙ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. мҝјлҰ¬ мҲҳн–үмқҙ мӢңмһ‘лҗҳл©ҙ AQEлҠ” мІҳмқҢм—җлҠ” лӘЁл“  Leaf stagesлҘј мӢңмһ‘н•ңлӢӨ. мқҙлҹ¬н•ң stagesл“Өмқҙ materializationмқ„ лҒқлӮҙл©ҙ, л¬јлҰ¬м Ғ мҝјлҰ¬ кі„нҡҚм—җм„ң мў…лЈҢлҗң кІғмңјлЎң н‘ңмӢңн•ҳкі , мҷ„лЈҢлҗң stagesлЎңл¶Җн„° м–»мқҖ нҶөкі„м№ҳлҘј к°Җм§Җкі  мқҙнӣ„мқҳ л…јлҰ¬м Ғ кі„нҡҚмқ„ м—…лҚ°мқҙнҠё н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң мқҙлҹ¬н•ң мғҲлЎңмҡҙ нҶөкі„м№ҳм—җ кё°л°ҳн•ҙм„ң н”„л Ҳмһ„мӣҢнҒ¬лҠ” мҳөнӢ°л§Ҳмқҙм Җ, л¬јлҰ¬м Ғ н”Ңлһҳл„Ҳ, л¬јлҰ¬м Ғ мөңм Ғнҷ” лЈ°(мқјл°ҳм Ғмқё л¬јлҰ¬м Ғ лЈ°кіј AQEмқҳ м—¬лҹ¬ лЈ°мқ„ нҸ¬н•Ё-м•„лһҳ)мқ„ мҲҳн–үн•ҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark AQE(Adaptive Query Execution)

content: Dynamically coalescing shuffle partitions м…”н”Ң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗмҷҖ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲлҠ” мҝјлҰ¬ м„ұлҠҘм—җ л§Өмҡ° м§ҒкІ°лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ л„Ҳл¬ҙ нҒ¬кұ°лӮҳ, мһ‘мңјл©ҙ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ л¬ём ңк°Җ л°ңмғқн•  мҲҳ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ: нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҙ л„Ҳл¬ҙ мһ‘мқ„ л•Ң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҙ л„Ҳл¬ҙ нҒҙ л•Ң л№„нҡЁмңЁм Ғмқё I/O Garbage Collection л¶Җн•ҳ мҠӨмјҖмӨ„лҹ¬мқҳ мҳӨлІ„н—Өл“ң Disk Spilling Task м…Ӣм—… мҳӨлІ„н—Өл“ң Dynamically coalescing shuffle partitions кё°лҠҘмқҖ лҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң м…”н”Ң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲҳлҘј мӨ„мқј мҲҳ мһҲлҸ„лЎқ н•ҳм—¬, кё°мЎҙмқҳ м •м Ғ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗ м„Өм • кё°лҠҘм—җм„ң л°ңмғқн•ҳлҠ” кІғкіј к°ҷмқҙ л„Ҳл¬ҙ нҒ¬кұ°лӮҳ мһ‘мқҖ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲмқҳ л¬ём ңлҘј н”јн•ҳл©° м„ұлҠҘмқ„ к°ңм„ н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. мөңмҙҲмқҳ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗлҠ” нҒ° лҚ°мқҙн„° мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲлҘј к°җлӢ№н•  мҲҳ мһҲлҸ„лЎқ нҒ¬кІҢ м„Өм •н•ҳкі , мҝјлҰ¬ stateм—җм„ң н•„мҡ”н•ҳлӢӨл©ҙ мһҗлҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲҳлҘј мӨ„мқҙкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. кё°мЎҙмқҳ м •м Ғ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗ м„Өм •мқҖ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ мҙҲкё°м—җ м„Өм •лҗң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗлҘј лҒқк№Ңм§Җ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳкё°м—җ мһ‘мқҖ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲк°Җ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ: н•ҳм§Җл§Ң, AQEк°Җ м Ғмҡ©лҗң кІҪмҡ°м—җлҠ” Coalescingмқҙ мһҗлҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң мҲҳн–үлҗҳм–ҙ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲ«мһҗк°Җ лҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң м„Өм •лҗҳм–ҙ лӮ®м•„м§Җл©° мһ‘мқҖ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲмқҳ л¬ём ңлҘј н”јн•  мҲҳ мһҲкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark AQE(Adaptive Query Execution)

content: Dynamically switching join strategies мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬лҠ” joinмқҳ лҢҖмғҒмқё 2к°ңмқҳ лҚ°мқҙн„° мӨ‘ н•ҳлӮҳ мқҙмғҒмқҙ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬м—җ лЎңл“ңлҗҳкё°м—җ м¶©л¶„нһҲ мһ‘лӢӨл©ҙ Broadcast Hash Joinмқ„ м„ нғқн•©лӢҲлӢӨ. н•ҳм§Җл§Ң, мҲҳн–ү мҙҲкё° мӢңм җм—җ н–үн•ң лҚ°мқҙн„° нҒ¬кё°м—җ лҢҖн•ң мҳҲмёЎмқҙ нӢҖлҰҙ мҲҳ мһҲмңјл©° мқҙлҠ” Broadcast Hash JoinлЎң мҲҳн–үлҗ  мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” кё°нҡҢлҘј лҶ“м№ҳкІҢ л§Ңл“ӯлӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҹ¬н•ң нӢҖлҰ° мҳҲмёЎмқҖ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҖмқҖ мқҙмң лЎң л°ңмғқн•ҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ: Cardinality лҳҗлҠ” Selectivity мҳҲмёЎмқ„ мң„н•ң нҶөкі„к°Җ л¶Җм •нҷ•н•  мҲҳ мһҲмқҢ лҢҖмғҒ лҚ°мқҙн„°к°Җ м—¬лҹ¬ мҳӨнҚјл Ҳмқҙн„°мқҳ ліөмһЎн•ң м„ңлёҢ нҠёлҰ¬мқј мҲҳ мһҲмқҢ UDFsмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ лё”лһҷл°•мҠӨ predicatesмқҙм—¬м„ң мҙҲкё° кё°м җм—җ нҶөкі„м№ҳ кі„мӮ°мқҙ л¶Ҳк°ҖлҠҘн•  мҲҳ мһҲмқҢ AQEмқҳ Dynamically switching join strateges кё°лҠҘмқҖ лҹ°нғҖмһ„ мӢңмқҳ м •ліҙлҘј л°”нғ•мңјлЎң join м „лһөмқ„ лӢӨмӢң кі„нҡҚн•  мҲҳ мһҲлҸ„лЎқ н•©лӢҲлӢӨ. мң„мҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ мҙҲкё°мқҳ Sort Merge Join м „лһөмқҙ, лҹ°нғҖмһ„ мӢң м •ліҙк°Җ м—…лҚ°мқҙнҠё лҗҳм–ҙ нҠ№м • лҚ°мқҙн„° лҢҖмғҒмқҙ м¶©л¶„нһҲ мһ‘лӢӨлҠ” мӮ¬мӢӨмқ„ мқём§Җн•ҳкІҢ лҗҳкі  Broadcast Hash JoinлЎң ліҖкІҪлҗҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark AQE(Adaptive Query Execution)

content: Dynamically optimizing skew joins AQEмқҳ 3лІҲм§ё кё°лҠҘмқҖ лҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң skewк°Җ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳлҠ” joinмқ„ мөңм Ғнҷ”н•ҳлҠ” кё°лҠҘмһ…лӢҲлӢӨ. лҚ°мқҙн„° skewлҠ” м…”н”Ң мӢңм—җ нҠ№м • нӮӨм—җ к°’мқҙ м№ҳмҡ°міҗм ём„ң л§Һмқҙ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳкі  м…”н”Ң мӢңм—җлҠ” н•ҙлӢ№ м…”н”Ңмқҳ лі‘л ¬ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ лӘЁл‘җ мў…лЈҢлҗҳкі  лӮҳм„ң лӢӨмқҢ лӢЁкі„мқҳ мІҳлҰ¬лҘҙ мҲҳн–үн•  мҲҳ мһҲкё°м—җ лӘЁл“  лі‘л ¬ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ л№ЁлҰ¬ лҒқлӮҳлҸ„ лӢӨмқҢ лӢЁкі„лЎң л„ҳм–ҙк°Җкё° мң„н•ҙм„ң к°ҖмһҘ лҠҰмқҖ мІҳлҰ¬лҘј кё°лӢӨлҰҙ мҲҳ л°–м—җ м—ҶкІҢ л§Ңл“ӯлӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҰ¬кі  мқҙ л¶Җл¶„мқҖ м„ұлҠҘмқ„ л§Өмҡ° м Җн•ҳмӢңнӮӨкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. AQEмқҳ Dynamically optimizing skew joins кё°лҠҘмқҖ лҹ°нғҖмһ„ нҶөкі„м№ҳлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲлЎңл¶Җн„° skewлҘј л””л үнҢ…н•ҳкі  skew нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқ„ лҚ” мһ‘мқҖ м„ңлёҢ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳл“ӨлЎң лӮҳлҲ•лӢҲлӢӨ. мң„мҷҖ к°ҷмқҖ join мӢңм—җ, мҳӨлҘёмӘҪкіј к°ҷмқҙ skew readerк°Җ skew нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ мЎҙмһ¬ м—¬л¶ҖлҘј нҢҢм•…н•ҳкі  мЎҙмһ¬н•ңлӢӨл©ҙ м„ңлёҢ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқ„ мғқм„ұн•ҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ мӮ¬мқҙмҰҲлҘј мӨ‘м җм ҒмңјлЎң мӮҙнҺҙліҙл©ҙ, мқјл°ҳм Ғмқё кІҪмҡ° A0 нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳм—җ лҚ”мҡұ мғҒлҢҖм ҒмңјлЎң л§ҺмқҖ лҚ°мқҙн„°к°Җ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳкі , A1, A2, A3 нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ лӘЁл‘җ лҒқлӮҳлҸ„ лӢӨмқҢ лӢЁкі„мқҳ мҲҳн–үмқ„ мң„н•ҙм„ң A0 мІҳлҰ¬ мў…лЈҢлҘј кё°лӢӨлҰ¬кІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ. AQEк°Җ м Ғмҡ©лҗҳкІҢ лҗҳл©ҙ м•„лһҳмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ лҸҷм ҒмңјлЎң skew нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқ„ мӘјк°ң лі‘л ¬мІҳлҰ¬н•ҳкІҢ лҗҳл©ҙм„ң, лҚ”мҡұ л№ лҘҙкІҢ м…”н”Ң лӢЁкі„мқҳ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ мҲҳн–үлҗҳкІҢ лҗ©лӢҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark AQE(Adaptive Query Execution)

content: Terms Term Meainig Application User program built on Spark. Consists of a driver program and executors on the cluster. Application jar A jar containing the userвҖҷs Spark application. In some cases users will want to create an вҖңuber jarвҖқ containing their application along with its dependencies. The userвҖҷs jar should never include Hadoop or Spark libraries, however, these will be added at runtime. Driver program The process running the main() function of the application and creating the SparkContext Cluster manager An external service for acquiring resources on the cluster (e.g. standalone manager, Mesos, YARN, Kubernetes) Deploy mode Distinguishes where the driver process runs. In cluster mode, the framework launches the driver inside of the cluster. In client mode, the submitter launches the driver outside of the cluster. Worker node Any node that can run application code in the cluster Executor A process launched for an application on a worker node, that runs tasks and keeps data in memory or disk storage across them. Each application has its own executors. Task A unit of work that will be sent to one executor Job A parallel computation consisting of multiple tasks that gets spawned in response to a Spark action (e.g. save, collect) Stage Each job gets divided into smaller sets of tasks called stages that depend on each other (similar to the map and reduce stages in MapReduce) Spark Architecture SparkлҠ” master / slave кө¬мЎ°лЎң 1к°ңмқҳ Driver(мӨ‘м•ҷ мЎ°м •мһҗ)мҷҖ Nк°ңмқҳ Executor(л¶„мӮ° мһ‘м—… л…ёл“ң)лЎң кө¬м„ұлҗҳм–ҙмһҲлӢӨ. Clusterм—җм„ң л¶„мӮ° лӘЁл“ңлЎң мӢӨн–үлҗңлӢӨл©ҙ н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ spark applicationмқҖ cluter manager(Standalone, YARN, Kubernetes)лқјкі  л¶ҲлҰ¬лҠ” мҷёл¶Җ м„ңл№„мҠӨлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙм„ң м—¬лҹ¬ к°ңмқҳ лЁёмӢ м—җм„ң мӢӨн–үлҗңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Cluster manager Cluster managerлҠ” м—¬лҹ¬ м„ңлІ„лЎң кө¬м„ұлҗң нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„° нҷҳкІҪм—җм„ң м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳл“Өмқҙ мһҳ лҸҷмһ‘н•  мҲҳ мһҲлҸ„лЎқ мһҗмӣҗмқ„ кҙҖлҰ¬н•ңлӢӨ. cluster manager мў…лҘҳм—җлҠ” нҒ¬кІҢ standalone, YARN, Kubernetesк°Җ мһҲлӢӨ. Spark runtime components Cluter deploy mode Client deploy mode Figure 1 Figure 2 Figure 1: Spark runtime components in Cluster deploy mode. Elements of a spark application are in blue boxes and an applicationвҖҷs tasks running inside task slots are labeled with a вҖңTвҖқ. Unoccupied task of slots are in white boxes. Figure 2: Spark runtime compoents in Client deploy mode. The driver is running inside the clientвҖҷs JVM process. The physical placement of executor and driver processes depends on the cluster type and its configuration. For example, some of these processes could share a single physical machine, or they could run on different ones. Figure 1 shows only the logical components in cluster deploy mode.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Responsibilities of the client process compoent The client process starts the driver program. For example, the client process can be a spark-submit script for running applications, a spark-shell script, or a custom application using Spark API. The client process prepares the classpath and all configuration options for the Spark application. It also passes application arguments, if any, to the application running inside the driver.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Responsibilities of the driver component The driver orchestrates and monitors execution of a Spark application. ThereвҖҷs always one driver per Spark application. You can think of the driver as a wrapper around the application. The driver and its subcomponents - the Spark context and scheduler - are responsible for: requesting memory and CPU resources from cluster managers breaking application logic into stages and tasks sending tasks to executors collecting the results
title: Spark Architecture

content: Two basic ways the driver program can be run are: Cluster mode: the driver process runs as a seperate JVM process inside a cluster, and the cluster manages its resources(mostly JVM heap memory). Client mode: the driverвҖҷs running inside the clientвҖҷs JVM process and communicates with the executors managed by the cluster.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Responsibilities of the executors The executors, which JVM processes, accept tasks from the driver, execute those tasks, and return the results to driver. Each executor has several task slots (or CPU cores) for running tasks in parallel. The executors in the figures have six tasks slots each. Those slots in white boxes are vacant. You can set the number of task slots to a value two or three times the number of CPU cores. Although these task slots are often referred to as CPU cores in Spark, theyвҖҷre implemented as threads and donвҖҷt need to correspond to the number of physical CPU cores on the machine.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Creation of the Spark Context Once the driver is started, it configures an instance of SparkContext. When running a Spark REPL shell, the shell is the driver program. Your Spark context is already preconfigured and available as a sc variable. When running running a standalone Spark applicaion by submitting a jar file, or by using Spark API from another program, yout Spark application starts and configures the Spark context. There can be only on Spark context per JVM. A Spark context comes with many useful methods for creating RDDs, loading data, and is the main interface for accessing Spark runtime. Local mode VS Deploy mode local modeмҷҖ deploy modeлҘј м„ нғқн•ҳлҠ” кё°мӨҖмқҖ clusterлҘј мӮ¬мҡ© м—¬л¶ҖлӢӨ. мҰү, clusterлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙм„ң л¶„мӮ° лӘЁл“ңлЎң м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳмқ„ мӢӨн–үн•ңлӢӨл©ҙ deploy mode, к·ёл Үм§Җ м•ҠлӢӨл©ҙ local modeлӢӨ. clusterлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ңлӢӨл©ҙ cluster manager мў…лҘҳмҷҖ л°°нҸ¬ л°©мӢқмқ„ м„ нғқн•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. cluster managerлҠ” standalone, YARN, Mesos л“ұ мӨ‘м—җ м„ нғқн•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. л°°нҸ¬ л°©мӢқм—җлҠ” client, clusterк°Җ мһҲкі  м–ҙл–Ө л°©мӢқмқ„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳлҠҗлғҗм—җ л”°лқј driver мӢӨн–ү мң„м№ҳк°Җ лӢ¬лқјм§„лӢӨ. мҰү, мҡ°лҰ¬лҠ” spark м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳмқ„ мӢӨн–үн• л•Ң local mode, client mode, cluster mode мҙқ 3к°Җм§Җ л°©мӢқмңјлЎң мӢӨн–үн•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Local mode local modeлҠ” local client JVMм—җ Driver 1к°ңмҷҖ Executor 1к°ңлҘј мғқм„ұн•ҳлҠ” нҳ•нғңлЎң, нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„°лҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡкі  лЎңм»¬ лӢЁмқј лЁёмӢ м—җм„ң м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳмқ„ мӢӨн–үн•ңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Client mode Client modeлҠ” Driverк°Җ Cluster мҷёл¶Җмқё Client JVMм—җм„ң мӢӨн–үлҗңлӢӨ. Client н”„лЎңм„ёмҠӨм—җ Driver programкіј к·ё м•Ҳм—җ Spark application, Spark Contextк°Җ мһҲкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ Client н”„лЎңм„ёмҠӨлҘј мӨ‘м§ҖмӢңнӮӨл©ҙ мҲҳн–ү мӨ‘мқҙлҚҳ лӘЁл“  мҠӨнҢҢнҒ¬ JobлҸ„ мў…лЈҢлҗңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Architecture

content: Cluster mode Cluster modeлҠ” Driverк°Җ Cluster лӮҙл¶Җмқҳ Worker node мӨ‘ н•ҳлӮҳм—җм„ң мӢӨн–үлҗңлӢӨ. м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳмқҖ Cluster лӮҙл¶Җм—җм„ң лҸ…лҰҪм Ғмқё н”„лЎңм„ёмҠӨлЎң мӢӨн–үлҗҳкі  Cluster managerм—җ мқҳн•ҙ мЎ°м •лҗҳкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ н•ҙлӢ№ лӘЁл“ңм—җм„ң ClientлҠ” м• н”ҢлҰ¬мјҖмқҙм…ҳ м ңм¶ң нӣ„ к°ңмһ…н•ҳм§Җ м•ҠлҠ”лӢӨ.
title: Spark Architecture

content: What is Partition? PartitionмқҖ RDDsлӮҳ Datasetмқ„ кө¬м„ұн•ҳкі  мһҲлҠ” мөңмҶҢ лӢЁмң„ к°қмІҙмқҙлӢӨ. к°Ғ PartitionмқҖ м„ңлЎң лӢӨлҘё л…ёл“ңм—җм„ң л¶„мӮ° мІҳлҰ¬лҗңлӢӨ. Sparkм—җм„ңлҠ” н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ мөңмҶҢ м—°мӮ°мқ„ Taskлқјкі  н‘ңнҳ„н•ҳлҠ”лҚ°, мқҙ н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Taskм—җм„ң н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Partitionмқҙ мІҳлҰ¬лҗңлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң, н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ TaskлҠ” н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Coreк°Җ м—°мӮ° мІҳлҰ¬н•ңлӢӨ. мҰү, 1 Core = 1 Task = 1 PartitionмқҙлӢӨ. мҳҲлҘј л“Өм–ҙ, лӢӨмқҢкіј к°ҷлӢӨл©ҙ м „мІҙ Core мҲҳлҘј 300к°ңлЎң м„ёнҢ…н•ң мғҒнғңмқҙкі , мқҙ 300к°ңк°Җ нҳ„мһ¬ мӢӨн–ү мӨ‘мқё Task мҲҳмқҙмһҗ, нҳ„мһ¬ мІҳлҰ¬ мӨ‘мқё Partition мҲҳм—җ н•ҙлӢ№н•ңлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң, м „мІҙ Partition мҲҳлҠ” 1800к°ңлЎң м„ёнҢ…н–Ҳмңјл©°, мқҙлҠ” м „мІҙ Task мҲҳмқҙкё°лҸ„ н•ҳлӢӨ. мқҙмІҳлҹј м„Өм •лҗң Partition мҲҳм—җ л”°лқј к°Ғ Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ кІ°м •лҗңлӢӨ. к·ёлҰ¬кі  мқҙ Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ кІ°көӯ Core лӢ№ н•„мҡ”н•ң л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ нҒ¬кё°лҘј кІ°м •н•ңлӢӨ. Partion мҲҳ -> Core мҲҳ Partition нҒ¬кё° -> л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ нҒ¬кё° л”°лқјм„ң, Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°мҷҖ мҲҳк°Җ Spark м„ұлҠҘм—җ нҒ° мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҜём№ҳлҠ”лҚ°, нҶөмғҒм ҒмңјлЎңлҠ” Partition нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒҙмҲҳлЎқ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ лҚ” н•„мҡ”н•ҳкі , Partitionмқҳ мҲҳк°Җ л§Һмқ„ мҲҳлЎқ Coreк°Җ лҚ” н•„мҡ”н•ҳлӢӨ. м ҒмқҖ мҲҳмқҳ Partition = нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒ° Partition л§ҺмқҖ мҲҳмқҳ Partition = нҒ¬кё°к°Җ мһ‘мқҖ Partition мҰү, Partitionмқҳ мҲҳлҘј лҠҳлҰ¬лҠ” кІғмқҖ Task лӢ№ н•„мҡ”н•ң л©”лӘЁлҜёлҘј мӨ„мқҙкі  лі‘л ¬нҷ”мқҳ м •лҸ„лҘј лҠҳлҰ°лӢӨ. Spark Partition мў…лҘҳ лҸҷмқјн•ң Partitionмқҙм§Җл§Ң, м“°мқҙлҠ” л•Ңм—җ л”°лқј лӢӨмқҢмқҳ 3к°Җм§ҖлЎң кө¬л¶„н•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. Input Partition Output Partition Shuffle Partition мқҙ мӨ‘, Sparkмқҳ мЈјмҡ” м—°мӮ°мқҙ Shuffleмқё л§ҢнҒј, Shuffle Partitionмқҙ к°ҖмһҘ мӨ‘мҡ”н•ҳлӢӨ.
title: Spark Partition

content: Input Partition кҙҖл Ё м„Өм •: spark.sql.files.maxpartitionBytes Input PartitionмқҖ мІҳмқҢ нҢҢмқјмқ„ мқҪмқ„ л•Ң мғқм„ұн•ҳлҠ” PartitionмқҙлӢӨ. кҙҖл Ё м„Өм •к°’мқҖ spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytesлЎң, Input Partition нҒ¬кё°лҘј м„Өм •н•  мҲҳ мһҲкі , кё°ліёк°’мқҖ 134217728(128MB)мқҙлӢӨ. нҢҢмқј(HDFS мғҒмқҳ л§Ҳм§Җл§ү кІҪлЎңм—җ мЎҙмһ¬н•ҳлҠ” нҢҢмқј)мқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ 128MBліҙлӢӨ нҒ¬лӢӨл©ҙ, Sparkм—җм„ң 128MBл§ҢнҒј мӘјк°ңл©ҙм„ң нҢҢмқјмқ„ мқҪлҠ”лӢӨ. нҢҢмқјмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ 128MBліҙлӢӨ мһ‘лӢӨл©ҙ к·ёлҢҖлЎң мқҪм–ҙ л“Өм—¬, нҢҢмқј н•ҳлӮҳлӢ№ Partition н•ҳлӮҳк°Җ лҗңлӢӨ. лҢҖл¶Җл¶„мқҳ кІҪмҡ°, н•„мҡ”н•ң м№јлҹјл§Ң кіЁлқјм„ң лҪ‘м•„ м“°кё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ 128MBліҙлӢӨ мһ‘лӢӨ. к°ҖлҒ”м”© нҒ° нҢҢмқјмқ„ лӢӨлЈ° кІҪмҡ°м—җлҠ” мқҙ м„Өм •к°’мқ„ мЎ°м Ҳн•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Partition

content: Output Partition кҙҖл Ё м„Өм •: df.repartition(cnt), df.coalesce(cnt) Output PartitionмқҖ нҢҢмқјмқ„ м ҖмһҘн•  л•Ң мғқм„ұн•ҳлҠ” PartitionмқҙлӢӨ. мқҙ Partitionмқҳ мҲҳк°Җ HDFS мғҒмқҳ л§Ҳм§Җл§ү кІҪлЎңмқҳ нҢҢмқј мҲҳлҘј м§Җм •н•ңлӢӨ. кё°ліём ҒмңјлЎң, HDFSлҠ” нҒ° нҢҢмқјмқ„ лӢӨлЈЁлҸ„лЎқ м„Өкі„лҗҳм–ҙ мһҲм–ҙ, нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒ° нҢҢмқјлЎң м ҖмһҘн•ҳлҠ” кІғмқҙ мўӢлӢӨ. ліҙнҶө HDFS Blocksizeм—җ л§һкІҢ м„Өм •н•ҳл©ҙ лҗҳлҠ”лҚ°, м№ҙм№ҙмҳӨ Hadoop нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„°мқҳ HDFS BlocksizeлҠ” 268435456 (256MB)лЎң м„Өм •лҗҳм–ҙ мһҲм–ҙм„ң, нҶөмғҒм ҒмңјлЎң нҢҢмқј н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј 256MBм—җ л§һлҸ„лЎқ Partitionмқҳ мҲҳлҘј м„Өм •н•ҳл©ҙ лҗңлӢӨ. Partitionмқҳ мҲҳлҠ” df.repartition(cnt), df.coalesce(cnt)лҘј нҶөн•ҙ м„Өм •н•ңлӢӨ. мқҙ repartitionкіј coalesceлҘј мқҙмҡ©н•ҙ Partition мҲҳлҘј мӨ„мқј мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. м•„лһҳмқҳ мҳҲмӢңлҠ”, нҢҢмқј мҲҳлҘј мӨ„м—¬ 50к°ңлЎң м ҖмһҘн•ҳлҠ” лӘЁмҠөмқҙлӢӨ. Use Case ліҙнҶө groupBy м§‘кі„ нӣ„ м ҖмһҘн•  л•Ң лҚ°мқҙн„°мқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ мһ‘м•„м§‘лӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҹ° лӢӨмқҢ spark.sql.shuffle.partitions м„Өм •м—җ л”°лқј нҢҢмқј мҲҳк°Җ м§Җм •лҗҳлҠ”лҚ°, мқҙл•Ң нҢҢмқјмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј лҠҳлҰ¬кё° мң„н•ҙ repartitionмҷҖ coalesceмқ„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙ Partition мҲҳлҘј мӨ„мқј мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. df.where()лҘј нҶөн•ҙ н•„н„°л§Ғмқ„ н•ҳкі  лӮҳм„ң к·ёлҢҖлЎң м ҖмһҘн•ңлӢӨл©ҙ нҢҢнҺёнҷ”к°Җ мғқк№ҒлӢҲлӢӨ. к·ёлһҳм„ң repartition(cnt)мқ„ н•ң нӣ„ м ҖмһҘн•ңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Partition

content: Shuffle Partition кҙҖл Ё м„Өм •: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions Spark м„ұлҠҘм—җ к°ҖмһҘ нҒ° мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҜём№ҳлҠ” PartitionмңјлЎң, Join, groupBy л“ұмқҳ м—°мӮ°мқ„ мҲҳн–үн•  л•Ң Shuffle Partitionмқҙ м“°мқёлӢӨ. м„Өм •к°’мқҖ spark.sql.shuffle.partitionsмқҙкі , мқҙ м„Өм •к°’м—җ л”°лқј Join, groupBy мҲҳн–ү мӢң Partitionмқҳ мҲҳ(лҳҗлҠ” Taskмқҳ мҲҳ)к°Җ кІ°м •лҗңлӢӨ. мқҙ м„Өм •к°’мқҖ Core мҲҳм—җ л§һкІҢ м„Өм •н•ҳлқјкі  н•ҳм§Җл§Ң, Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°м—җ л§һм¶”м–ҙ м„Өм •н•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. мқҙ Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°к°Җ нҒ¬кі  м—°мӮ°м—җ м“°мқҙлҠ” л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ л¶ҖмЎұн•ҳлӢӨл©ҙ Shuffle Spill(лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј м§Ғл ¬нҷ”н•ҳкі  мҠӨнҶ лҰ¬м§Җм—җ м ҖмһҘ, мІҳлҰ¬ мқҙнӣ„м—җлҠ” м—ӯм§Ғл ¬нҷ”н•ҳкі  м—°мӮ° мһ¬к°ңн•Ё)мқҙ л°ңмғқн•ҳкё° л•Ңл¬ёмқҙлӢӨ. Shuffle Spillмқҙ л°ңмғқн•ҳл©ҙ, Taskк°Җ м§Җм—°лҗҳкі  м—җлҹ¬к°Җ л°ңмғқн•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң, Hadoop нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„°мқҳ мӮ¬мҡ©лҘ мқҙ лҶ’лӢӨл©ҙ, м—°лӢ¬м•„ м—җлҹ¬к°Җ л°ңмғқн•ҳкі  Sparkк°Җ к°•м ң мў…лЈҢлҗ  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. Memory Limit OverмҷҖ к°ҷмқҙ, Shuffle SpillлҸ„ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ л¶ҖмЎұмңјлЎң лӮҳнғҖлӮҳлҠ”лҚ°, ліҙнҶө мқҙм—җ лҢҖн•ң лҢҖмқ‘мқ„ Core лӢ№ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬лҘј лҠҳлҰ¬лҠ” кІғмңјлЎң н•ҙкІ°н•ңлӢӨ. н•ҳм§Җл§Ң, лӘЁл“  мӮ¬лһҢмқҙ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ л¶ҖмЎұн•ҳлӢӨкі  л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ н• лӢ№лҹүмқ„ лҠҳлҰ°лӢӨл©ҙ, нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„°к°Җ мӮ¬мҡ©м„ұмқҙ лҚ” л–Ём–ҙм§Җкі  мһ‘м—…мқҙ лҚ”мҡұ мӢӨнҢЁн•  кІғмқҙлӢӨ. к·ёлһҳм„ң к°ңмқём ҒмңјлЎң, Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј кІ°м •н•ҳлҠ” мҳөм…ҳмқё Spark.sql.shuffle.partitionsлҘј мҡ°м„ м ҒмңјлЎң кі л Өн•ҙ м„Өм •н•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. лҳҗн•ң, мқјл°ҳм ҒмңјлЎң н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ Shuffle Partition нҒ¬кё°к°Җ 100 ~ 200MB м •лҸ„ лӮҳмҳ¬ мҲҳ мһҲлҸ„лЎқ spark.sql.shuffle.partitions мҲҳлҘј мЎ°м Ҳн•ҳлҠ” кІғмқҙ мөңм ҒмқҙлӢӨ. Use Case Memory Limit Over, Memory Spill л“ұ мһҗмӣҗ л¬ём ңк°Җ мғқкёё кІҪмҡ°, Shuffle Partition нҒ¬кё°лҘј мҡ°м„ м ҒмңјлЎң кі л Өн•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. Partition мөңм Ғнҷ” мөңм Ғнҷ”мқҳ мҡ°м„  мҲңмң„лҠ” мҝјлҰ¬ > Partition мҲҳ > Core лӢ№ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ мҰқк°ҖмқҙлӢӨ. мҝјлҰ¬лҠ” мөңлҢҖн•ң groupByлЎң м§‘кі„лҘј н•ң нӣ„ joinмқ„ н•ҳкі  к·ё лӢӨмқҢм—җ Partition мҲҳлҘј мЎ°м Ҳн•ң лӢӨмқҢ, к·ёлһҳлҸ„ м•ҲлҗңлӢӨл©ҙ Core лӢ№ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬лҘј мҰқк°ҖмӢңмјңм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. Partition мҲҳлҘј мҰқк°ҖмӢңнӮЁлӢӨл©ҙ Task мҲҳлҸ„ лҠҳм–ҙлӮҳм„ң мӢӨн–ү мӢңк°„мқҙ мҰқк°Җлҗ  мҲҳ мһҲм§Җл§Ң, Shuffle Spillмқҙ мқјм–ҙлӮҳм§Җ м•ҠлҸ„лЎқ н•ңлӢӨл©ҙ мӢңк°„мқҙ лҚ” к°җмҶҢн•ңлӢӨ. л”°лқјм„ң Shuffle Spillмқҙ мқјм–ҙлӮҳм§Җ м•ҠкІҢ н•ҳлҠ” м„ мқё Shuffle Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј 100 ~ 200MBлЎң м„Өм •н•ҳлҠ” кІғмқҙ мөңм ҒмқҙлӢӨ. лӢЁ, лҢҖл¶Җл¶„мқҳ лҚ°мқҙн„° мІҳлҰ¬м—җм„ң мң„мқҳ м„Өм •мқҙ м Ғн•©н•ҳм§Җл§Ң, Shuffle Sizeк°Җ 600GBм—җ к°Җк№қкұ°лӮҳ к·ё мқҙмғҒмқј кІҪмҡ°м—җлҠ” Core лӢ№ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬лҘј мҰқк°ҖмӢңнӮӨлҠ” кІғмқ„ к¶ҢмһҘн•ңлӢӨ. ліҙнҶө Shuffle Sizeк°Җ 600GB мқҙмғҒмқҙ лҗҳл©ҙ 1 мҪ”м–ҙлӢ№ 4GBлҘј кі л Өн•ҳлҠ” кІғмқ„ к¶ҢмһҘн•ңлӢӨ. Cartesian join(cross join) мӮ¬мҡ©мңјлЎң Row мҲҳк°Җ кёүкІ©н•ҳкІҢ мҰқк°Җн•ң кІҪмҡ°м—җлҸ„ Shuffle Sizeк°Җ м»Өм§Җкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ мҰқк°ҖлҘј кі л Өн•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. мқҙ мҷём—җлҸ„, Spark MLмқ„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳкұ°лӮҳ Cachingмқ„ н•ҳлҠ” кІҪмҡ°, Spark л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ кө¬мЎ° мӨ‘ Storage Memory Fraction л¶Җл¶„м—җм„ң мәҗмӢұмқ„ н•ҳкІҢ лҗҳлҠ”лҚ°, мқҙл ҮкІҢ лҗҳл©ҙ м—°мӮ°(Execution)мқ„ н•ҙм•ј н•ҳлҠ” л¶Җл¶„мқҙ мӨ„м–ҙл“Өм–ҙ кІ°көӯм—җлҠ” л©”лӘЁлҰ¬лҘј мҰқлҢҖн•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. Storage л©”лӘЁлҰ¬: Sparkмқҳ Cache лҚ°мқҙн„° м ҖмһҘмқ„ мң„н•ҙ мӮ¬мҡ© Execution л©”лӘЁлҰ¬: Shuffle, Join, Sort, Aggregation л“ұмқҳ м—°мӮ° кіјм •м—җм„ң мһ„мӢң лҚ°мқҙн„° м ҖмһҘмқ„ мң„н•ҙ мӮ¬мҡ© м •лҰ¬ Shuffle Spillмқҙ мқјм–ҙлӮңлӢӨл©ҙ м—җлҹ¬к°Җ л°ңмғқн•ҙ мһ‘м—…мқҙ м§Җм—°лҗ  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. к·ёлҰ¬кі  Hadoop нҒҙлҹ¬мҠӨн„°к°Җ busy мғҒнғңмқё кІҪмҡ°, м—°лӢ¬м•„ м—җлҹ¬к°Җ л°ңмғқн•ҳкі  к°•м ң мў…лЈҢлҗ  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. л©”лӘЁлҰ¬к°Җ л¶ҖмЎұн•ҳлӢӨл©ҙ, мҡ°м„ м ҒмңјлЎң Shuffle Partition мҲҳлҘј кі л Өн•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ. Shuffle Partitionмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј 100MB~200MB мӮ¬мқҙлЎң лӮҳмҳӨлҸ„лЎқ spark.sql.Shuffle.PartitionsлҘј м„Өм •н•ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨ.
title: Spark Partition

content: What is YAML? YAML is a programming language that is frequently combined with a wide range of other languages and is mostly used for creating configuration files. When creating configuration files for application projects, languages like Python and the Google-developed Flutter framework for Dart both use YAML (.yaml). Since it can be used to format containerized files, YAML is also well-liked among cloud engineers. Types

content: Join? JOINмқҖ лҚ°мқҙн„°лІ мқҙмҠӨ лӮҙмқҳ м—¬лҹ¬ н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җм„ң к°Җм ёмҳЁ л ҲмҪ”л“ңлҘј мЎ°н•©н•ҳм—¬ н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлӮҳ кІ°кіј м§‘н•©мңјлЎң н‘ңнҳ„н•ҙ мЈјлҠ” кІғмқҙлӢӨ. Inner join: көҗм§‘н•© Left / Right join: л¶Җл¶„ м§‘н•© Outer Join: н•©м§‘н•© мҳҲмӢң лҚ°мқҙн„° ID ENAME 1 AAA 2 BBB 3 CCC ID KNAME 1 к°Җ 2 лӮҳ 4 лқј 5 л§Ҳ
title: SQL Join

content: Inner Join мЎ°мқён•ҳлҠ” н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҳ ON м Ҳмқҳ мЎ°кұҙмқј мқјм№ҳн•ҳлҠ” кІ°кіјл§Ң м¶ңл Ҙ select u.userid, name from usertbl as u inner join buytbl as b on u.userid=b.userid where u.userid="111" -- joinмқ„ мҷ„лЈҢн•ҳкі  к·ёлӢӨмқҢ мЎ°кұҙмқ„ л”°м§„лӢӨ. SELECT A.ID, A.ENAME, A.KNAME FROM A INNER JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID ID ENAME KNAME 1 AAA к°Җ 2 BBB лӮҳ
title: SQL Join

content: Left / Right Outer Join л‘җ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙ н•©міҗ м§Ҳл•Ң мҷјмӘҪ/мҳӨлҘёмӘҪмқ„ кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң н–ҲлҠҗлғҗм—җ л”°лқј кё°мӨҖ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҳ кІғмқҖ лӘЁл‘җ м¶ңл Ҙлҗҳм–ҙм•ј н•ңлӢӨкі  мқҙн•ҙн•ҳл©ҙ лҗңлӢӨ. OUTER JOINмқҖ мЎ°мқён•ҳлҠ” н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҳ ON м Ҳмқҳ мЎ°кұҙ мӨ‘ н•ңмӘҪмқҳ лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј лӘЁл‘җ к°Җм ёмҳЁлӢӨ OUTER JOINмқҖ LEFT OUTER JOIN, RIGHT OUTER JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN мқҙл ҮкІҢ 3к°Җм§Җк°Җ мһҲлӢӨ. LEFT OUTER JOINмқ„ кұ°мқҳ лҢҖл¶Җл¶„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм—¬, FULL OUTER JOINмқҖ м„ұлҠҘмғҒ кұ°мқҳ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм§Җ м•ҠлҠ”лӢӨ.
title: SQL Join

content: Left Outer Join LEFT JOINмқҖ л‘җ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙ мһҲмқ„ кІҪмҡ°, мІ« лІҲм§ё н…Ңмқҙлё”мқ„ кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң л‘җ лІҲм§ё н…Ңмқҙлё”мқ„ мЎ°н•©н•ҳлҠ” JOINмқҙлӢӨ. -- мҳҲ) 1н•ҷл…„ н•ҷмғқмқҳ мқҙлҰ„кіј м§ҖлҸ„көҗмҲҳлӘ…мқ„ м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқј. -- лӢЁ, м§ҖлҸ„көҗмҲҳк°Җ м§Җм •лҗҳм§Җ м•ҠмқҖ н•ҷмғқлҸ„ м¶ңл ҘлҗҳкІҢ н•ҳлқј. SELECT STUDENT.NAME, PROFESSOR.NAME FROM STUDENT LEFT OUTER JOIN PROFESSOR -- STUDENTлҘј кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң мҷјмӘҪ мЎ°мқё ON STUDENT.PID = PROFESSOR.ID WHERE GRADE = 1 SELECT A.ID, A.ENAME, A.KNAME FROM A LEFT OUTER JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID ID ENAME KNAME 1 AAA к°Җ 2 BBB лӮҳ 3 CCC NULL
title: SQL Join

content: Right Outer Join RIGHT JOINмқҖ л‘җ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙ мһҲмқ„ кІҪмҡ°, л‘җ лІҲм§ё н…Ңмқҙлё”мқ„ кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң мІ« лІҲм§ё н…Ңмқҙлё”мқ„ мЎ°н•©н•ҳлҠ” JOINмқҙлӢӨ. -- мҳҲ) 1н•ҷл…„ н•ҷмғқмқҳ мқҙлҰ„кіј м§ҖлҸ„көҗмҲҳлӘ…мқ„ м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқј. -- лӢЁ, м§ҖлҸ„көҗмҲҳк°Җ м§Җм •лҗҳм§Җ м•ҠмқҖ н•ҷмғқлҸ„ м¶ңл ҘлҗҳкІҢ н•ҳлқј. SELECT STUDENT.NAME, PROFESSOR.NAME FROM STUDENT RIGHT OUTER JOIN PROFESSOR -- PROFESSORлҘј кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң мҳӨлҘёмӘҪ мЎ°мқё ON STUDENT.PID = PROFESSOR.ID WHERE GRADE = 1 SELECT A.ID, A.ENAME, A.KNAME FROM A RIGHT OUTER JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID ID ENAME KNAME 1 AAA к°Җ 2 BBB лӮҳ 4 NULL лқј 5 NULL л§Ҳ
title: SQL Join

content: Exclusive Left / Right Join
title: SQL Join

content: Exclusive Left Join м–ҙлҠҗ нҠ№м • н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җ мһҲлҠ” л ҲмҪ”л“ңл§Ң к°Җм ёмҳӨлҠ” кІғ вҖӢEXCLUSIVE JOINлҠ” л§Ңм•Ҫ н…Ңмқҙлё” л‘җк°ңлҘј JOINн•ңлӢӨл©ҙ л‘ҳмӨ‘ н•ңк°Җм§Җ н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җл§Ң мһҲлҠ” лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј к°Җм ёмҳЁлӢӨ. лӢӨлҘё JOINл“ӨкіјлҠ” лӢӨлҘҙкІҢ лі„лҸ„мқҳ EXCLUSIVE JOINн•ЁмҲҳк°Җ мһҲлҠ” кІғмқҖ м•„лӢҲкі  кё°мЎҙмқҳ LEFT JOINкіј Whereм Ҳмқҳ мЎ°кұҙмқ„ н•Ёк»ҳ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм—¬ л§Ңл“ңлҠ” JOIN л¬ёлІ•мқҙлӢӨ. -- мЎ°мқён•ң B н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҳ к°’мқҙ nullл§Ң м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқјлҠ” л§җмқҖ, -- мЎ°мқёмқҙ м•Ҳлҗң A л ҲмҪ”л“ң лӮҳлЁём§Җк°’л§Ң м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқјлҠ” л§җ SELECT * FROM table1 A LEFT JOIN table2 B ON A.ID_SEQ = B.ID_SEQ WHERE B.ID_SEQ IS NULL SELECT A.ID, A.ENAME, A.KNAME FROM A LEFT OUTER JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID WHERE B.ID IS NULL ID ENAME KNAME 3 CCC NULL
title: SQL Join

content: Exclusive Right Join м–ҙлҠҗ нҠ№м • н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җ мһҲлҠ” л ҲмҪ”л“ңл§Ң к°Җм ёмҳӨлҠ” кІғ вҖӢEXCLUSIVE JOINлҠ” л§Ңм•Ҫ н…Ңмқҙлё” л‘җк°ңлҘј JOINн•ңлӢӨл©ҙ л‘ҳмӨ‘ н•ңк°Җм§Җ н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җл§Ң мһҲлҠ” лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј к°Җм ёмҳЁлӢӨ. лӢӨлҘё JOINл“ӨкіјлҠ” лӢӨлҘҙкІҢ лі„лҸ„мқҳ EXCLUSIVE JOINн•ЁмҲҳк°Җ мһҲлҠ” кІғмқҖ м•„лӢҲкі  кё°мЎҙмқҳ RIGHT JOINкіј Whereм Ҳмқҳ мЎ°кұҙмқ„ н•Ёк»ҳ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм—¬ л§Ңл“ңлҠ” JOIN л¬ёлІ•мқҙлӢӨ. -- мЎ°мқён•ң A н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҳ к°’мқҙ nullл§Ң м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқјлҠ” л§җмқҖ, -- мЎ°мқёмқҙ м•Ҳлҗң B л ҲмҪ”л“ң лӮҳлЁём§Җк°’л§Ң м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳлқјлҠ” л§җ SELECT * FROM table1 A RIGHT JOIN table2 B ON A.ID_SEQ = B.ID_SEQ WHERE A.ID_SEQ IS NULL SELECT A.ID, A.ENAME, A.KNAME FROM A RIGHT OUTER JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID WHERE A.ID IS NULL ID ENAME KNAME 4 NULL лқј 5 NULL л§Ҳ
title: SQL Join

content: Self Join -- мҳҲ) лӘЁл“  мӮ¬мӣҗм—җ лҢҖн•ҙ мӮ¬мӣҗмқҳ мқҙлҰ„кіј м§ҒмҶҚ мғҒмӮ¬мқҳ мқҙлҰ„мқ„ кІҖмғү н•ҙлқј. -- EMPNAME н…Ңмқҙлё”м—җ м–ҙл–Ө мӮ¬мӣҗмқҳ MANAGER лІҲнҳёк°Җ к°ҷмқҖ -- н…Ңмқҙлё” лӮҙм—җм„ң м–ҙл–Ө мӮ¬мӣҗмқҳ EMPNOмҷҖ к°ҷмңјл©ҙ к·ё мӮ¬мӣҗмқҙ м§ҒмҶҚ мғҒкҙҖ SELECT E.EMPNAME as мӮ¬мӣҗ, M.EMPNAME as м§ҒмҶҚмғҒкҙҖ FROM EMPLOYEE E, EMPLOYEE M -- inner join WHERE E.MANAGER = M.EMPNO;
title: SQL Join

content: Union UNIONмқҖ м—¬лҹ¬ к°ңмқҳ SELECT л¬ёмқҳ кІ°кіјлҘј н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлӮҳ кІ°кіј м§‘н•©мңјлЎң н‘ңнҳ„н•  л•Ң мӮ¬мҡ© мқҙл•Ң к°Ғк°Ғмқҳ SELECT л¬ёмңјлЎң м„ нғқлҗң н•„л“ңмқҳ к°ңмҲҳмҷҖ нғҖмһ…мқҖ лӘЁл‘җ к°ҷм•„м•ј н•ҳл©°, н•„л“ңмқҳ мҲңм„ң лҳҗн•ң к°ҷм•„м•ј н•ңлӢӨ. кё°ліё м§‘н•© мҝјлҰ¬м—җлҠ” (DISTINCT) мӨ‘ліөм ңкұ°к°Җ мһҗлҸҷ нҸ¬н•Ёлҗҳм–ҙмһҲлӢӨ. SELECT н•„л“ңмқҙлҰ„ FROM н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлҰ„ UNION SELECT н•„л“ңмқҙлҰ„ FROM н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлҰ„
title: SQL Join

content: Union All UNIONмқҖ DISTINCT мһҗлҸҷ нҸ¬н•Ёмқҙлқј мӨ‘ліөлҗҳлҠ” л ҲмҪ”л“ңлҘј м ңкұ°н•ңлӢӨ. л”°лқјм„ң мӨ‘ліөлҗҳлҠ” л ҲмҪ”л“ңк№Ңм§Җ лӘЁл‘җ м¶ңл Ҙн•ҳкі  мӢ¶лӢӨл©ҙ, ALL нӮӨмӣҢл“ңлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳл©ҙ лҗңлӢӨ. SELECT н•„л“ңмқҙлҰ„ FROM н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлҰ„ UNION ALL SELECT н•„л“ңмқҙлҰ„ FROM н…Ңмқҙлё”мқҙлҰ„

content: na мӮ¬мҡ© # лӘЁл“  Column к°’мқҙ NULLмқё кІғ мӮӯм ң. Dfn.na.drop(how="all") # м–ҙл–Ө Columnd мқҙлҚҳ NULL мқё кІғ мӮӯм ң. Dfn.na.drop("any") # firstname && lastnameмқҙ NULLмқё кІғ мӮӯм ң. Dfn.na.drop("all", Seq("firstname", "lastname")) # firstname || lastnameмқҙ NULL мқё кІғ мӮӯм ң. Dfn.na.drop("any", Seq("firstname", "lastname"))
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: na & fill мӮ¬мҡ© # лӘЁл“  NULL к°’м—җ abcdefg мұ„мӣҢмӨҖлӢӨ. # мЈјмқҳн•  м җ) Typeмқҙ мқјм№ҳн•ҙм•ј мұ„мӣҢмӨ„ мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. # abcedfg(String) != birthyear(Datetime)мқҙкё°м—җ к·ёлҢҖлЎң NULLлЎң лӮЁм•„мһҲлӢӨ. Dfn.na.fill("abcdefg") # нҠ№м • Columnмқҙ NULL к°’мқј л•Ң нҠ№м • к°’мқ„ мұ„мӣҢмӨ„ мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. Dfn.na.fill(Map( "salutation" -> "Unknown", "firstname" -> "John", "lastname" -> "Doe", "bithyear" -> 9999 ))
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: Sort and Order Rows - Sort & OrderBy # NULL к°’мқҙ м—ҶлҠ” лҚ°мқҙн„°л“Өмқ„ # firstnameмқ„ мҳӨлҰ„м°ЁмҲң м •л ¬ # monthлҘј лӮҙлҰјм°ЁмҲң м •л ¬ # sortмҷҖ orderByлҠ” м„ңлЎң м—°кҙҖмқҙ м—Ҷкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ # мөңмў…м ҒмңјлЎңлҠ” firstname м •л ¬мқҖ м Ғмҡ©лҗҳм§Җ м•Ҡкі  monthл§Ң м Ғмҡ©лҗңлӢӨ. customerDf .na.drop("any") .sort("firstname") == .sort(expr("firstname")) .orderBy(expr("month(birthdate)").desc) .select("firstname", "lastname", "birthdate") # firtname мҳӨлҰ„м°ЁмҲң & lastname лӮҙлҰјм°ЁмҲңмңјлЎң м •л ¬н•ҳл Өл©ҙ лӢӨмқҢкіј к°ҷлӢӨ. customerDf .na.drop("any") .sort($"firstname", $"lastname".desc) .select("firstname", "lastname", "birthdate")
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: Joining DataFrames - Inner Join Address DF address_id country state city zip street_name street_number location_type 1 United States AZ FairField 86192 Jackson 18 condo 2 United States NM Fairview 85709 Washington 6th 362 condo Customer DF address_id birth_country birthdate customer_id demographics 2133 RWANDA 1935-09-13 45721 вҖңbuy_potentialвҖқ: вҖң10000вҖқ, вҖңcredint_ratingвҖқ: вҖңHigh RistвҖқ, вҖңeducation_statusвҖқ: вҖңSecondaryвҖқ # Apache Sparkмқҳ default JOIN = Inner customerWithAddress = customerDf.join( addressDf, customerDf.col("address_id") === addressDf.col("address_id"), "inner" # мғқлһө к°ҖлҠҘ ) .select( "customer_id", "address_id", "demographics.education_status", "location_type", "country", "city", "street_name" )
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: Joining DataFrames - Right Outer Join Right tableмқ„ кё°мӨҖмңјлЎң Left tableм—җм„ң мқјм№ҳн•ҳлҠ” к°’л“Өмқ„ л§Өн•‘ н•ҙмӨҖлӢӨ. л§Ңм•Ҫ Left tableм—җм„ң мқјм№ҳн•ҳлҠ” к°’мқҙ м—ҶлӢӨл©ҙ NULLмқ„ л„ЈлҠ”лӢӨ. webSalesDf.join( customerDf, "customer_id" === "ws_bill_customer_sk", "right" ) .select( "customer_id", "ws_bill_customer_sk", ) .where( "ws_bill_customer_sk is null" )
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: Joining DataFrames - Left Outer Join webSalesDf.join( customerDf, $"customer_id" === $"ws_bill_customer_sk", "left" )
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: Appending Rows to a DataFrame - Union df1 = customerDf.select( "firstname", "lastname", "customer_id" ) .withColumn( "from", lit("df1") ) df2 = customerDf.select( "lastname", "firstname", "customer_id" ) .withColumn( "from", lit("df2") ) # df2 мқҳ лӘЁл“  лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј df1м—җ н•©міҗмӨҖлӢӨ. # к°ҷмқҖ лҚ°мқҙн„° мһ„м—җлҸ„ column мң„м№ҳлҘј кё°л°ҳмңјлЎң н•©м№ҳкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ лӢӨлҘё кІғмңјлЎң мқёмӢқн•ңлӢӨ. df1.union(df2) firstname lastname customer_id from Tiffany Skinner 45721 df1 Skinner Tiffany 45721 df2 df1.unionByName(df2) firstname lastname customer_id from Tiffany Skinner 45721 df1 Tiffany Skinner 45721 df2 # distinct() мҷҖ кІ°н•©н•ҙм„ң мӨ‘ліөмқ„ м—Ҷм• мӨҖлӢӨ. df1.unionByName(df2).distinct() firstname lastname customer_id Tiffany Skinner 45721 Tiffany Skinner 45721
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: DataFrameWriter I customerWithAddress = customerDf .na.drop("any") .join( addressDf, customerDf.address_id == addressDf.address_id, ) .select( 'customer_id', 'demographics', concat_ws(" ", "firstname", "lastname").as("Name") addressDf("*") ) salesWithItem = webSalesDf .na.drop("any") .join( itemDf, webSalesDf.ws_item_sk == itemDf.i_item_sk, ) .selectExpr( "ws_bill_customer_sk customer_id", "ws_ship_addr_sk ship_address_id", ) # defaultлЎң partitionмқҖ 200мқҙлӢӨ. # л¶Ҳн•„мҡ”н•ҳкІҢ л§ҺмқҖ кІҪмҡ°, 8к°ңлЎң лӮ®м¶°мӨҖлӢӨ. # нҢҢмқј нҳ•мӢқмқҖ jsonмңјлЎң н•ҙмӨҖлӢӨ. # SaveMode.OverwriteлЎң лҳ‘к°ҷмқҖ нҢҢмқјмқ„ лҚ®м–ҙмҚЁмӨҖлӢӨ. customerWithAddress .repartition(8) .write .format("json") .mode(SaveMode.Overwrite) .option("path", "tmp/output/customerWithAddress") .save
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: DataFrameWriter II - PartitionBy Partitions the output by the given columns on the file system. customerWithAddress .repartition(8) .write .partitionBy("item_category") .format("json") .mode(SaveMode.Overwrite) .option("path", "tmp/output/customerWithAddress") .save

content: Repartition VS Coalesce RDDлҘј мғқм„ұн•ң л’Ө filter() м—°мӮ°мқ„ л№„лЎҜн•ң лӢӨм–‘н•ң transformation м—°мӮ°мқ„ мҲҳн–үн•ҳлӢӨліҙл©ҙ мөңмҙҲм—җ м„Өм •лҗң partition к°ңмҲҳк°Җ м Ғн•©н•ҳм§Җ м•ҠмқҖ кІҪмҡ°к°Җ л°ңмғқн•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. мқҙ кІҪмҡ° coalesce()лӮҳ repartition()м—°мӮ°мқ„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙ нҳ„мһ¬мқҳ RDDмқҳ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ к°ңмҲҳлҘј мЎ°м •н•  мҲҳ мһҲлӢӨ. л‘җ л©”м„ңл“ңлҠ” лӘЁл‘җ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ нҒ¬кё°лҘј лӮҳнғҖлӮҙлҠ” м •мҲҳлҘј мқёмһҗлЎң л°ӣм•„м„ң нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳмқҳ мҲҳлҘј мЎ°м •н•ңлӢӨлҠ” м җм—җм„ң кіөнҶөм җмқҙ мһҲм§Җл§Ң repartition()мқҙ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲҳлҘј лҠҳлҰ¬кұ°лӮҳ мӨ„мқҙлҠ” кІғмқ„ лӘЁл‘җ н•  мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” л°ҳл©ҙ coalesce()лҠ” мӨ„мқҙлҠ” кІғл§Ң к°ҖлҠҘн•ҳлӢӨ. к·ёлҹј лӘЁл“  кІғмқҙ к°ҖлҠҘн•ң repartition() л©”м„ңл“ңк°Җ мһҲмқҢм—җлҸ„ coalesce() л©”м„ңл“ңлҘј л”°лЎң л‘җлҠ” мқҙмң лҠ” л°”лЎң мІҳлҰ¬ л°©мӢқм—җ л”°лҘё м„ұлҠҘ м°Ёмқҙ л•Ңл¬ёмқҙлӢӨ. мҰү repartition()мқҖ м…”н”Ңмқ„ кё°л°ҳмңјлЎң лҸҷмһ‘мқ„ мҲҳн–үн•ҳлҠ” лҚ° л°ҳн•ҙ coalesce()лҠ” к°•м ңлЎң м…”н”Ңмқ„ мҲҳн–үн•ҳлқјлҠ” мҳөм…ҳмқ„ м§Җм •н•ҳм§Җ м•ҠлҠ” н•ң м…”н”Ңмқ„ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡкё° л•Ңл¬ёмқҙлӢӨ. л”°лқјм„ң лҚ°мқҙн„° н•„н„°л§Ғ л“ұмқҳ мһ‘м—…мңјлЎң лҚ°мқҙн„° мҲҳк°Җ мӨ„м–ҙл“Өм–ҙ нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲҳлҘј мӨ„мқҙкі мһҗ н•  л•ҢлҠ” мғҒлҢҖм ҒмңјлЎң м„ұлҠҘмқҙ мўӢмқҖ coalesce()лҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳкі , нҢҢнӢ°м…ҳ мҲҳлҘј лҠҳл Өм•ј н•ҳлҠ” кІҪмҡ°м—җл§Ң repartition() л©”м„ңл“ңлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳлҠ” кІғмқҙ мўӢлӢӨ.
title: Spark DataFrame API

content: What is Kubernetes or K8s Kubernetes also known as K8s was built by Google based on their expreience running containers in production. It is now an open-source project and is arguably one of the best and most pupular contaniner orchestrations technologies out there. To understand Kubernetes, we must first understand two things - Container and Orchestration. Containers
title: Kubernetes

content: Why do you need containers? In one of my previos projects, I had this requirement to setup an end-to-end stack including various different technologies like a Web Server using NodeJS and database such as MongoDB/CouchDB, messaging system like Redis and an orchestration tool like Ansible. We had a lot of issues developing this application with all these different components. First, their compatibility with the underlying OS. We had to ensure that all these different services were compatible with the version of the OS we were planning to use. There have been times when certain version of these services were not compatible with the OS, and we have had to go back and look for another OS that was compatible with all of these different services. Secondly, we had to check the compatibility between these services and the libraries and dependencies on the OS. We have had issues were one service requires one version of a dependent library whereas another service required another version. The architecture of our application changed over time, we have had to upgrade to newer versions of these components, or change the database etc and everytime something changed we had to go through the same process of checking compatibility between these various components and the underlying infrastructure. This compatibility matrix issue is usually referred to as the matrix from hell. Next, everytime we had a new developer on board, we found it really difficult to setup a new environment. The new developers had to follow a large set of instructions and run 100s of commands to finally setup their environments. They had to make sure they were using the right Operating System, the right versions of each of these components and each developer had to set all that up by himself each time. We also had different development test and production environments. One developer may be comfortable using one OS, and the others may be using another one and so we couldnвҖҷt gurantee the application that we were building would run the same way in different environments. And So all of this made our life in developing, building and shipping the application really difficult.
title: Kubernetes

content: What can it do? So I needed something that could help us with the compatibility issue. And something that will allow us to modify or change these components without affecting the other components and even modify the underlying operating systems as required. And that search landed me on Docker. With Docker I was able to run each component in a separate container вҖ“ with its own libraries and its own dependencies. All on the same VM and the OS, but within separate environments or containers. We just had to build the docker configuration once, and all our developers could now get started with a simple вҖңdocker runвҖқ command. Irrespective of what underlying OS they run, all they needed to do was to make sure they had Docker installed on their systems.
title: Kubernetes

content: What is Containers? Containers are completely isolated enviroments, as in they can have their own processes or services, their own network interfaces, their own mounts, just like Virtual machines, except that they all share the same OS Kernel. W But its also important to note that containers are not new with Docker. Containers have existed for about 10 years now and some of the different types of containers are LXC, LXD, LXCFS etc. Docker utilized LXC and that is were Docker offeres a high-level tool with several powerful functionalities making it really easy for end users like us.
title: Kubernetes

content: Operating system To understand how Docker works let us revisit some basics concepts of Operating Systems first. If you look at operating systems like Ubuntu, Fedora, Suse or Centos вҖ“ they all consist of two things. An OS Kernel and a set of software. The OS Kernel is responsible for interacting with the underlying hardware. While the OS kernel remains the sameвҖ“ which is Linux in this case, itвҖҷs the software above it that make these Operating Systems different. This software may consist of a different User Interface, drivers, compilers, File managers, developer tools etc. SO you have a common Linux Kernel shared across all Oses and some custom softwares that differentiate Operating systems from each other.
title: Kubernetes

content: Sharing the Kernel We said earlier that Docker containers share the underlying kernel. What does that actually mean вҖ“ sharing the kernel? LetвҖҷs say we have a system with an Ubuntu OS with Docker installed on it. Docker can run any flavor of OS on top of it as long as they are all based on the same kernel вҖ“ in this case Linux. If the underlying OS is Ubuntu, docker can run a container based on another distribution like debian, fedora, suse or centos. Each docker container only has the additional software, that we just talked about in the previous slide, that makes these operating systems different and docker utilizes the underlying kernel of the Docker host which works with all Oses above. So what is an OS that do not share the same kernel as these? Windows ! And so you wont be able to run a windows based container on a Docker host with Linux OS on it. For that you would require docker on a windows server. You might ask isnвҖҷt that a disadvantage then? Not being able to run another kernel on the OS? The answer is No! Because unlike hypervisors, Docker is not meant to virtualize and run different Operating systems and kernels on the same hardware. The main purpose of Docker is to containerize applications and to ship them and run them.
title: Kubernetes

content: Containers VS Virtual Machines So that brings us to the differences between virtual machines and containers. Something that we tend to do, especially those from a Virtualization. As you can see on the right, in case of Docker, we have the underlying hardware infrastructure, then the OS, and Docker installed on the OS. Docker then manages the containers that run with libraries and dependencies alone. In case of a Virtual Machine, we have the OS on the underlying hardware, then the Hypervisor like a ESX or virtualization of some kind and then the virtual machines. As you can see each virtual machine has its own OS inside it, then the dependencies and then the application. This overhead causes higher utilization of underlying resources as there are multiple virtual operating systems and kernel running. The virtual machines also consume higher disk space as each VM is heavy and is usually in Giga Bytes in size, wereas docker containers are lightweight and are usually in Mega Bytes in size. This allows docker containers to boot up faster, usually in a matter of seconds whereas VMs we know takes minutes to boot up as it needs to bootup the entire OS. It is also important to note that, Docker has less isolation as more resources are shared between containers like the kernel etc. Whereas VMs have complete isolation from each other. Since VMs donвҖҷt rely on the underlying OS or kernel, you can run different types of OS such as linux based or windows based on the same hypervisor.
title: Kubernetes

content: How is it done? There are a lot of containerized versions of applications readily available as of today. So most organizations have their products containerized and available in a public docker registry called dockerhub/or docker store already. For example you can find images of most common operating systems, databases and other services and tools. Once you identify the images you need and you install Docker on your host bringing up an application stack, is as easy as running a docker run command with the name of the image. In this case running a docker run ansible command will run an instance of ansible on the docker host. Similarly run an instance of mongodb, redis and nodejs using the docker run command. And then when you run nodejs just point to the location of the code repository on the host. If we need to run multiple instances of the web service, simply add as many instances as you need, and configure a load balancer of some kind in the front. In case one of the instances was to fail, simply destroy that instance and launch a new instance.
title: Kubernetes

content: Container VS Image An image is a package or a template, just like a VM template that you might have worked with in the virtualization world. It is used to create one or more containers. Containers are running instances of images that are isolated and have their own enviroments and set of processes.
title: Kubernetes

content: Container Advantage Traditionally developers developed applications. Then they hand it over to Ops team to deploy and manage it in production environments. They do that by providing a set of instructions such as information about how the hosts must be setup, what pre-requisites are to be installed on the host and how the dependencies are to be configured etc. Since the Ops team did not develop the application on their own, they struggle with setting it up. When they hit an issue, they work with the developers to resolve it. With Docker, a major portion of work involved in setting up the infrastructure is now in the hands of the developers in the form of a Docker file. The guide that the developers built previously to setup the infrastructure can now easily put together into a Dockerfile to create an image for their applications. This image can now run on any container platform and is guaranteed to run the same way everywhere. So the Ops team now can simply use the image to deploy the application. Since the image was already working when the developer built it and operations are not modifying it, it continues to work the same when deployed in production. Container Orchestration So we learned about containers and we now have our application packaged into a docker container. But what next? How do you run it in production? What if your application relies on other containers such as database or messaging services or other backend services? What if the number of users increase and you need to scale your application? You would also like to scale down when the load decreases. To enable these functionalities you need an underlying platform with a set of resources. The platform needs to orchestrate the connectivity between the containers and automatically scale up or down based on the load. This whole process of automatically deploying and managing containers is known as Container Orchestration.
title: Kubernetes

content: Orchestration Technologies Kubernetes is thus a container orchestration technology. There are multiple such technologies available today вҖ“ Docker has its own tool called Docker Swarm. Kubernetes from Google and Mesos from Apache. While Docker Swarm is really easy to setup and get started, it lacks some of the advanced autoscaling features required for complex applications. Mesos on the other hand is quite difficult to setup and get started, but supports many advanced features. Kubernetes - arguably the most popular of it all вҖ“ is a bit difficult to setup and get started but provides a lot of options to customize deployments and supports deployment of complex architectures. Kubernetes is now supported on all public cloud service providers like GCP, Azure and AWS and the kubernetes project is one of the top ranked projects in Github.
title: Kubernetes

content: Kubernetes Advantage There are various advantages of container orchestration. Your application is now highly available as hardware failures do not bring your application down because you have multiple instances of your application running on different nodes. The user traffic is load balanced across the various containers. When demand increases, deploy more instances of the application seamlessly and within a matter of second and we have the ability to do that at a service level. When we run out of hardware resources, scale the number of nodes up/down without having to take down the application. And do all of these easily with a set of declarative object configuration files. It is a container Orchestration technology used to orchestrate the deployment and management of 100s and 1000s of containers in a clustered environment. Architecture
title: Kubernetes

content: Nodes(Minions) A node is a machine вҖ“ physical or virtual вҖ“ on which kubernetes is installed. A node is a worker machine and this is were containers will be launched by kubernetes. It was also known as Minions in the past. So you might here these terms used inter changeably. But what if the node on which our application is running fails? Well, obviously our application goes down. So you need to have more than one nodes.
title: Kubernetes

content: Cluster A cluster is a set of nodes grouped together. This way even if one node fails you have your application still accessible from the other nodes. Moreover having multiple nodes helps in sharing load as well.
title: Kubernetes

content: Master Now we have a cluster, but who is responsible for managing the cluster? Were is the information about the members of the cluster stored? How are the nodes monitored? When a node fails how do you move the workload of the failed node to another worker node? ThatвҖҷs were the Master comes in. The master is another node with Kubernetes installed in it, and is configured as a Master. The master watches over the nodes in the cluster and is responsible for the actual orchestration of containers on the worker nodes.
title: Kubernetes

content: Components When you install Kubernetes on a System, you are actually installing the following components. An API Server. An ETCD service. A kubelet service. A Container Runtime, Controllers and Schedulers. The API server acts as the front-end for kubernetes. The users, management devices, Command line interfaces all talk to the API server to interact with the kubernetes cluster. Next is the ETCD key store. ETCD is a distributed reliable key-value store used by kubernetes to store all data used to manage the cluster. Think of it this way, when you have multiple nodes and multiple masters in your cluster, etcd stores all that information on all the nodes in the cluster in a distributed manner. ETCD is responsible for implementing locks within the cluster to ensure there are no conflicts between the Masters. The scheduler is responsible for distributing work or containers across multiple nodes. It looks for newly created containers and assigns them to Nodes. The controllers are the brain behind orchestration. They are responsible for noticing and responding when nodes, containers or endpoints goes down. The controllers makes decisions to bring up new containers in such cases. The container runtime is the underlying software that is used to run containers. In our case it happens to be Docker. And finally kubelet is the agent that runs on each node in the cluster. The agent is responsible for making sure that the containers are running on the nodes as expected.
title: Kubernetes

content: Master VS Work Nodes So far we saw two types of servers вҖ“ Master and Worker and a set of components that make up Kubernetes. But how are these components distributed across different types of servers. In other words, how does one server become a master and the other slave? The worker node (or minion) as it is also known, is were the containers are hosted. For example Docker containers, and to run docker containers on a system, we need a container runtime installed. And thatвҖҷs were the container runtime falls. In this case it happens to be Docker. This doesnвҖҷt HAVE to be docker, there are other container runtime alternatives available such as Rocket or CRIO. The master server has the kube-apiserver and that is what makes it a master. Similarly the worker nodes have the kubelet agent that is responsible for interacting with the master to provide health information of the worker node and carry out actions requested by the master on the worker nodes. All the information gathered are stored in a key-value store on the Master. The key value store is based on the popular etcd framework as we just discussed. The master also has the controller manager and the scheduler.
title: Kubernetes

content: Kubectl And finally, we also need to learn a little bit about ONE of the command line utilities known as the kube command line tool or kubectl or kube control as it is also called. The kube control tool is used to deploy and manage applications on a kubernetes cluster, to get cluster information, get the status of nodes in the cluster and many other things. The kubectl run command is used to deploy an application on the cluster. The kubectl cluster-info command is used to view information about the cluster and the kubectl get pod command is used to list all the nodes part of the cluster. Setup There are lots of ways to setup Kubernetes. We can setup it up ourselves locally on our laptops or virtual machines using solutions like Minikube and Kubeadmin. Minikube is a tool used to setup a single instance of Kubernetes in an All-in-one setup and kubeadmin is a tool used to configure kubernetes in a multi-node setup. There are also hosted solutions available for setting up kubernetes in a cloud environment such as GCP and AWS. And finally if you donвҖҷt have the resources or if you donвҖҷt want to go through the hassle of setting it all up yourself, and you simply want to get your hands on a kubernetes cluster instantly to play with, checkout play-with-k8s.com.
title: Kubernetes

content: Minikube We will start with Minikube which is the easiest way to get started with Kubernetes on a local system. Before we head into the demo itвҖҷs good to understand how it works. Earlier we talked about the different components of Kubernetes that make up a Master and worker nodes such as the api server, etcd key value store, controllers and scheduler on the master and kubelets and container runtime on the worker nodes. It would take a lot of time and effort to setup and install all of these various components on different systems individually by ourlselves. Minikube bundles all of these different components into a single image providing us a pre-configured single node kubernetes cluster so we can get started in a matter of minutes. The whole bundle is packaged into an ISO image and is available online for download. Now you donвҖҷt HAVE to download it yourself. Minikube provides an executable command line utility that will AUTOMATICALLY download the ISO and deploy it in a virtualization platform such as Oracle Virtualbox or Vmware fusion. So you must have a Hypervisor installed on your system. For windows you could use Virtualbox or Hyper-V and for Linux use Virtualbox or KVM. And finally to interact with the kubernetes cluster, you must have the kubectl kubernetes command line tool also installed on your machine. So you need 3 things to get this working, you must have a hypervisor installed, kubectl installed and minikube executable installed on your system. Setup - Kubeadm With the minikube utility you could only setup a single node kubernetes cluster. The kubeadmin tool helps us setup a multi node cluster with master and workers on separate machines. Installing all of these various components individually on different nodes and modifying the configuration files to make it work is a tedious task. Kubeadmin tool helps us in doing all of that very easily. First, you must have multiple systems or virtual machines created for configuring a cluster. Once the systems are created, designate one as master and others as worker nodes. The next step is to install a container runtime on the hosts. We will be using Docker, so we must install Docker on all the nodes. The next step is to install kubeadmin tool on all the nodes. The kubeadmin tool helps us bootstrap the kubernetes solution by installing and configuring all the required components in the right nodes. The third step is to initialize the Master server. During this process all the required components are installed and configured on the master server. That way we can start the cluster level configurations from the master server. Once the master is initialized and before joining the worker nodes to the master, we must ensure that the network pre-requisites are met. A normal network connectivity between the systems is not SUFFICIENT for this. Kubernetes requires a special network between the master and worker nodes which is called as a POD network. The last step is to join the worker nodes to the master node. We are then all set to launch our application in the kubernetes environment. Install How to install Kubernetes? How to install Virtual Box How to install minikube? Start Minikube with Docker Warning рҹҳ„ Darwin 14.4.1 (arm64) мқҳ minikube v1.33.0 вңЁ мң м Җ нҷҳкІҪ м„Өм • м •ліҙм—җ кё°л°ҳн•ҳм—¬ virtualbox л“ңлқјмқҙлІ„лҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳлҠ” мӨ‘ вқҢ Exiting due to DRV_UNSUPPORTED_OS: The driver 'virtualbox' is not supported on darwin/arm64 Apple M3 Pro м№© нҷҳкІҪмқҖ м•„м§Ғ Minikube - Virtual BoxлҘј м§Җмӣҗн•ҳм§Җ м•ҠлҠ” л“Ҝ н•ҳлӢӨ. н•  мҲҳ м—Ҷмқҙ DockerлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҙліёлӢӨ. {seilylook} рҹ”Ҙ оӮ° ~ оӮ° minikube start --driver=docker рҹҳ„ Darwin 14.4.1 (arm64) мқҳ minikube v1.33.0 вңЁ мң м Җ нҷҳкІҪ м„Өм • м •ліҙм—җ кё°л°ҳн•ҳм—¬ docker л“ңлқјмқҙлІ„лҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳлҠ” мӨ‘ рҹ“Ң Using Docker Desktop driver with root privileges рҹ‘Қ Starting "minikube" primary control-plane node in "minikube" cluster рҹҡң Pulling base image v0.0.43 ... рҹ’ҫ мҝ лІ„л„ӨнӢ°мҠӨ v1.30.0 мқ„ лӢӨмҡҙлЎңл“ң мӨ‘ ... > preloaded-images-k8s-v18-v1...: 319.81 MiB / 319.81 MiB 100.00% 31.20 M > gcr.io/k8s-minikube/kicbase...: 434.52 MiB / 434.52 MiB 100.00% 21.03 M рҹ”Ҙ Creating docker container (CPUs=2, Memory=4600MB) ... рҹҗі мҝ лІ„л„ӨнӢ°мҠӨ v
